model.label: GPT-5.2
model.description: OpenAI • Smaller GPT frontier model tuned for fast, cost-efficient reasoning and tool use.

pick.rationale: St. John’s has a clear overall quality edge in the efficiency metrics (KenPom #20 / Torvik #18) and is meaningfully better offensively than Northern Iowa, which matters if UNI’s strong defense can’t fully dictate tempo. Northern Iowa is a live 12-seed because it defends at a high level (top-30 AdjD) and can keep the game in the half court, but its offense grades well below St. John’s and leaves a thinner margin if it falls behind. With no specific, verified availability red flags surfaced in the quick scan, I’m siding with St. John’s higher two-way ceiling and better scoring reliability.

reasoningStep.title: East 5/12 — Lean favorite on two-way efficiency edge
reasoningStep.summary: St. John’s rates as the substantially stronger team overall and should have enough offense to separate even against UNI’s defense, though the underdog’s defensive profile makes this a non-trivial upset spot.
evidence: Ratings: St. John’s KenPom #20 (AdjEM +24.8; AdjO 119.9 #46; AdjD 95.1) and Torvik #18 (AdjO 119.43; AdjD 94.84 #12) vs Northern Iowa KenPom #71 (AdjEM +11.86; AdjO 110.1 #153; AdjD 98.2) and Torvik #70 (AdjO 109.66 #151; AdjD 98.54 #26).
Upset context: a national preview list flagged this as a plausible upset (The Athletic), consistent with UNI’s defensive path, but the offensive gap favors St. John’s in a one-game setting.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

Mitchell Layton / Getty Images By Jordan Brenner and Peter Keating March 16, 2026 Updated March 17, 2026 9:40 am EDT Share full article 22 The NCAA Tournament is all about upsets, and as doctors in this field of study, we’ve crunched the numbers and have assembled our annual list of likely threats and victims. McNeese, VCU and High Point: We’re sizing you up for glass slippers. UNC, Vanderbilt and Wisconsin? You might need some Rolaids. Advertisement Welcome to Year 20 of Bracket Breakers! If you’ve been following us this whole time, you know to expect a whole lot of upset-predicting math and even more puns in the coming days. If you’re new to the scene, here’s what to expect: We will provide deep, regional breakdowns of every first-round matchup with a difference of at least five seeds, powered by our upset-seeking model, Slingshot. You can read a detailed explanation of how the process works here . Want a closer look? Dig into our Definitive Guide to First-Round Upsets for in-depth explanations as to why our model projects this year’s upsets the way it does. But for now, here’s a quick look at the 10 most likely first-round upsets according to our model. No. 5 Vanderbilt Commodores vs. No. 12 McNeese Cowboys Upset Chance: 41.2 percent Last week, we identified the Cowboys as our model’s second-favorite potential Bracket Breaker . And since Santa Clara (our top choice) ended up seeded 10th, putting them in a game without a five-seed difference, McNeese now tops our chart. The Cowboys lead the country in forcing turnovers (24.9 percent of opponents’ possessions), and they grab offensive boards at a top-50 rate, which makes Slingshot positively swoon. Their upset odds would be even higher if not for the fact that Vandy profiles as an extremely safe favorite, or as we call them, a

Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
